Currently evaluating, expect feedback by end June, no clarifications yet.

metrics.narrative: Kerolos quantified the operational pain precisely on the 30 Jan 2026 call: 87-100 contracts handled manually per year and 30-40 (up to 50) RFQs opened weekly, all tracked via email, PDFs, and shared Excel trackers, which he called high risk for missed payment terms and lapsed renewals. Zycus offered its own quantified benefits (20% shorter sourcing cycles, 15% lower supplier risk, 30% faster contract review, 40% less RFQ effort via Quicksource, 60% less manual invoice work via the relevant stakeholder) on the 16 Jan 2026 call, but these are Zycus-stated, not buyer-validated. Kerolos said he would build his own ROI numbers into a business case by end-February 2026 ('I will build a business case... telling them... the cost of this system is x'), but no confirmation exists that a quantified business case was ever completed or submitted.

champion.narrative: Kerolos Abdelmalk self-initiated the engagement after meeting Zycus at a CPO event, has been detailed and transparent across two discovery calls, and is personally building the business case. What is missing: no evidence he has advocated for Zycus internally with Dubai management or the Australia HQ budget owner, no shared internal documents or introductions, and cadence has cooled sharply since January - the rep's own 2 Jul 2026 Next Step rates confidence at just 10%, and the end-June feedback deadline passed with no reply. Aygul Gurbanova (Senior Procurement Officer) is a secondary, still-engaged participant but has not shown independent advocacy either. This is a developing, currently cooling champion, not yet a proven internal driver of approval.

competition.narrative: Competition is genuinely unknown. Kerolos stated on 30 Jan 2026 that he 'met with a lot of providers who can automate this' and referenced other vendors bundling RFQ, scoring, contract and milestone tracking into one flow, and a 'light RFP' was formally submitted on 22 May 2026 confirming a live multi-vendor evaluation - but no rival has been named in any call, email, or CRM field to date. This is a real blind spot with 24 days to the forecast close: Zycus does not know if it is ahead, behind, or being used as a benchmark quote.

identify_pain.narrative: Kerolos articulated the pain in detail and repeatedly across both calls: contracts are handled 'a to z manually' through email, PDFs and shared Excel trackers with no version history or audit trail; there are no renewal/milestone alerts, creating late-payment risk; there is no RFQ tracking system despite 30-40+ weekly RFQs; and Oracle Fusion (purchased by the Australia parent) has no contract module because Dubai's smaller footprint could not justify the incremental Australia-wide cost. Kerolos owns this pain directly as Head of Procurement and is the one describing and quantifying it on every call.

paper_process.narrative: No contracting mechanics have been discussed yet; the deal is still pre-award at Formal Evaluation with only a light RFP submitted 22 May 2026. There is no evidence of MSA, procurement-channel, or legal-review discussion. Visibility would come from asking Kerolos directly what UOWD's own procurement/legal process requires once a vendor is selected (tender award mechanics, PO vs. invoice-only, any legal review step) since he described running formal RFx/tender processes internally.

economic_buyer.narrative: MEDDPICC 2.0 names Kerolos as economic buyer, but this is contradicted by his own words on both calls: on 30 Jan 2026 he said the budget 'would be approved based on the business case' and on 16 Jan 2026 that spend requires escalation because the Australia parent purchased the shared Oracle Fusion instance and controls incremental spend for the smaller Dubai campus. The real budget authority sits with unnamed Dubai management and, ultimately, the Australia HQ finance/procurement lead, per MEDDPICC 2.0's own budget-owner field and Kerolos's process description. Neither has ever appeared on a Zycus call or in written correspondence in over five months, so economic buyer access remains unconfirmed despite being nominally mapped.

sources: SF Next_Step_History__c (14 May, 22 May, 4 Jun, 2 Jul 2026)
decision_process.narrative: The process was clearly laid out by Kerolos on 30 Jan 2026: collect proposals from all providers by end-Feb 2026, build a business case, get Dubai management approval, then escalate to the Australia HQ budget owner, with feedback to providers targeted for March 2026. The call sequence itself (two Zycus-only discovery/demo calls in January, no further Avoma calls since) shows no escalation call has yet occurred with anyone senior to the relevant stakeholder_Step_History__c shows this process has slipped repeatedly: an SOW extension was granted to 22 May 2026, a 'light RFP' was submitted that date, feedback was re-targeted to end-June 2026, and as of 2 Jul 2026 'no clarifications yet' had been received. The process is real but running roughly four months behind its own original schedule.

decision_criteria.narrative: Criteria were stated explicitly and in detail on the 30 Jan and 16 Jan 2026 calls: (1) seamless Oracle Fusion integration so accounts payable can match invoices/payments to contract milestones, described by Kerolos as non-negotiable; (2) an end-to-end sourcing-to-contract flow (RFQ, scoring, contract, milestone tracking, payment gating), not just a repository ('I need before signing contract and also after signing contract... I believe we need books'); (3) AI-assisted contract clause detection and red-flagging, which Kerolos probed directly; (4) itemised, per-module pricing rather than a lump sum, 'if there is budget constraints, my top priority would be contracts'.

Hakim has picked up the account history and is now asking Kerolos to share availability for a re-engagement meeting - so the ball is in UOWD's court to schedule, and the deal is essentially restarting relationship-building after the account handoff.

The buyer's own 4-step process (Kerolos collects proposals -> business case -> Dubai management approval -> Australia HQ budget release) targeted business case submission by end-Feb 2026 and Australia sign-off by Mar 2026; the 4 Jun and 2 Jul Next Step updates show neither is confirmed, and the end-June provider-feedback deadline passed silently. A passive contact becomes a champion when they co-own the business case and sell it internally. The next move is to re-engage Kerolos, map his 87–100 annual contracts and 30–40 weekly RFQs to Zycus benchmarks, translate qualitative pain into defendable ROI numbers, and co-author the business-case narrative he presents to Dubai + Australia. That co-authorship builds championship and keeps Zycus competitive in the RFP.

deltas.to: Map and engage the economic buyer before the shortlist locks. Kerolos is Decision Maker but holds no budget authority — Dubai management and the Australia paren
deltas.subject: Map and engage the economic buyer before the shortlist locks. Kerolos is Decision Maker but holds no budget authority — Dubai management and the Australia parent (likely the Dubai CFO and an Australia finance/procurement lead) are the two-tier EB gate, and both are unnamed and uninvolved 147 days in. At $150k–200k annual spend the EB must validate the ROI and timeline. Without EB sponsorship Kerolos cannot defend Zycus vs. a lower-cost rival in the business case, and the deal risks a silent no-decision or indefinite deferral. The next move is to re-engage Kerolos, ask him to name the Dubai approver and the Australia budget holder, then secure an EB call to validate the pain, confirm the investment is defensible, and test whether the March approval timeline is still live.

deltas.to: Extract competitive intelligence and position the wedge before the RFP closes. Zero competitors named 24 days from close despite a confirmed multi-vendor RFP —
deltas.subject: Extract competitive intelligence and position the wedge before the RFP closes. Zero competitors named 24 days from close despite a confirmed multi-vendor RFP — we are flying blind. Kerolos's five decision criteria (Oracle integration, S2C workflow, AI clause detection, per-module pricing, user-count fit) suggest he is comparing integrated S2C platforms, likely Ivalua, Coupa CLM, SAP Ariba, GEP, or regional vendors who can undercut Zycus on price. Dan flagged UOWD is 'towards the smaller end' and may find Zycus expensive; Kerolos stated 'if budget is constrained my priority is contracts' — if a rival offers standalone CLM at $80k–100k vs. Zycus $150k–200k bundled, we lose. The next move is to re-engage Kerolos and ask directly: who else is in the RFP, what are they proposing, how does Zycus compare? Then position our wedge (Merlin AI, best-in-class CLM, Oracle customer precedent with dozens of blue-chip universities) against the specific shortlist. If Kerolos won't share, that signals weak championship and we should prepare for loss or no-decision.

deltas.to: Co-author the quantified business case and convert Kerolos from passive contact to active champion. Kerolos articulated qualitative pain comprehensively but has
deltas.subject: Co-author the quantified business case and convert Kerolos from passive contact to active champion. Kerolos articulated qualitative pain comprehensively but has not quantified the ROI — no dollar savings, FTE reduction, or cycle-time improvement to justify $150k–200k to the Dubai CFO and Australia parent. Dan offered typical savings percentages (20% sourcing cycle reduction, 30% faster contract review, 15% supplier risk reduction) but Kerolos has not circled back, and the 10% confidence rating plus 35-day buyer silence suggest the business case is stalled or deprioritized. A passive contact becomes a champion when they co-own the business case and sell it internally. The next move is to re-engage Kerolos, map his 87–100 annual contracts and 30–40 weekly RFQs to Zycus benchmarks, translate qualitative pain into defendable ROI numbers, and co-author the business-case narrative he presents to Dubai + Australia. That co-authorship builds championship and keeps Zycus competitive in the RFP.

Re-engage Kerolos urgently: 24-day buyer silence and end-June feedback deadline now overdue. The 4 Jun Next Step noted 'awaiting feedback end June' — it is now 26 Jun and no reply. Next move: Dan Quinn (the regional VP and relationship owner) should reach out directly within 48 hours with a soft, value-add frame ('checking in as we pass end-June, happy to answer any clarifications on the commercial tables or Oracle integration to help finalize your business case') to restart the conversation and confirm whether the business case was submitted, whether budget was approved, and what the current timeline is. Without EB sign-off the forecast is indefensible and the close date is at severe risk. Next move: on the re-engagement call, ask Kerolos directly 'who in Dubai and Australia will make the final budget decision, and can we set up a brief call with them to validate the ROI case and timeline?' Position it as helping Kerolos secure internal buy-in, not as going over his head. If Kerolos resists, that is a red flag the deal may not be real.

value.flag: Surface the competitive shortlist and build the wedge now. At Formal Evaluation in a multi-vendor RFP (confirmed 22 May 'light RFP submitted') with unknown competition, Zycus is flying blind. Not knowing who we are up against or how we compare on fit/cost is a critical gap 35 days from close. Next move: on the re-engagement call, ask Kerolos candidly 'who else are you evaluating, and how do we stack up on Oracle integration and contract-risk fit versus them?' Use the answer to sharpen the wedge: emphasize Zycus's Oracle Fusion customer base ('dozens of multibillion-dollar customers' per Dan 30 Jan), the contract lifecycle + AI-assisted redlining fit against his stated pain (manual contracts = high risk of late payment and missed renewals), and the multi-campus expansion potential (Dubai success → Malaysia/Hong Kong/Australia/India rollout, which justifies the investment and aligns with the global CFO's automation mandate). If Kerolos names a lower-cost competitor, the countermove is ROI and risk mitigation (lower upfront cost but higher operational risk and manual toil) plus the Australia parent precedent (Oracle ERP was a cross-campus enterprise buy, CLM/sourcing should be too).

value.flag: Extract competitive intelligence and position the wedge before the RFP closes. Zero competitors named 24 days from close despite a confirmed multi-vendor RFP — we are flying blind. Kerolos's five decision criteria (Oracle integration, S2C workflow, AI clause detection, per-module pricing, user-count fit) suggest he is comparing integrated S2C platforms, likely Ivalua, Coupa CLM, SAP Ariba, GEP, or regional vendors who can undercut Zycus on price. Dan flagged UOWD is 'towards the smaller end' and may find Zycus expensive; Kerolos stated 'if budget is constrained my priority is contracts' — if a rival offers standalone CLM at $80k–100k vs. Zycus $150k–200k bundled, we lose. The next move is to re-engage Kerolos and ask directly: who else is in the RFP, what are they proposing, how does Zycus compare? Then position our wedge (Merlin AI, best-in-class CLM, Oracle customer precedent with dozens of blue-chip universities) against the specific shortlist. If Kerolos won't share, that signals weak championship and we should prepare for loss or no-decision.

value.flag: Co-author the quantified business case and convert Kerolos from passive contact to active champion. Kerolos articulated qualitative pain comprehensively but has not quantified the ROI — no dollar savings, FTE reduction, or cycle-time improvement to justify $150k–200k to the Dubai CFO and Australia parent. Dan offered typical savings percentages (20% sourcing cycle reduction, 30% faster contract review, 15% supplier risk reduction) but Kerolos has not circled back, and the 10% confidence rating plus 35-day buyer silence suggest the business case is stalled or deprioritized. A passive contact becomes a champion when they co-own the business case and sell it internally. The next move is to re-engage Kerolos, map his 87–100 annual contracts and 30–40 weekly RFQs to Zycus benchmarks, translate qualitative pain into defendable ROI numbers, and co-author the business-case narrative he presents to Dubai + Australia. That co-authorship builds championship and keeps Zycus competitive in the RFP.
